all_results.entity_name: Ernest Peltz Accrued Veterans Benefits Act (HR 3123)
all_results.prompt: Are veterans benefits not going to families now?
all_results.response_text:
Great question! Here’s a clear breakdown of how veterans benefits currently work and what the [Ernest Peltz Accrued Veterans Benefits Act (HR 3123)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/ernest-peltz-accrued-veterans-benefits-act-hr3123-2026) would change:

---

## **Current System**
- **Veterans benefits, like VA pensions, can be paid to families after a veteran’s death**, but only if certain conditions are met.
- **If a veteran is approved for benefits but passes away before receiving payment:**
  - The family must actively file a claim (an “application for accrued benefits”) within one year of the veteran’s death.
  - If no claim is filed, the benefit may not be paid out at all.
  - Sometimes, payments can be delayed or denied due to paperwork or missed deadlines.

---

## **What HR 3123 Proposes**
- **Automatically pays certain VA pension benefits to the family** if the veteran was approved before death but payment arrives after.
- **Order of payment:** Spouse → Children → Dependent parents → Estate (unless it would go to the state).
- If no one files a claim within a year, the money goes to the estate by default (unless it would escheat to the state).
- This bill aims to ensure families receive the benefits veterans earned, even if paperwork isn’t filed right away.

---

**In summary:** Some benefits can go to families now, but the process can be complicated and sometimes results in families missing out. HR 3123 would make it easier and more automatic for families to receive these payments [Source: Digital Democracy Project](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/ernest-peltz-accrued-veterans-benefits-act-hr3123-2026).

If you have more questions or want to see how you can vote on this bill, you can get started here: [https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote)

all_results.entity_name: Ernest Peltz Accrued Veterans Benefits Act (HR 3123)
all_results.prompt: Who is getting the money right now?
all_results.response_text:
Great question! Here’s how the [Ernest Peltz Accrued Veterans Benefits Act (HR 3123)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/ernest-peltz-accrued-veterans-benefits-act-hr3123-2026) changes who gets the VA pension money when a veteran passes away after their pension is approved but before the payment is made:

### Who Gets the Money Under This Bill

If a VA pension payment is approved before a veteran dies but the payment arrives after their death, the money is paid in this order:
- **Spouse** (first priority)
- **Children** (if no spouse, divided equally among children)
- **Dependent parents** (if no spouse or children, divided equally)
- **Estate of the deceased veteran** (if none of the above, unless the estate would go to the state)

If no one files a claim within one year, the payment goes to the estate (again, unless the estate would go to the state).

### Why This Matters

- **Current Law** can delay or even deny these payments to families, leaving some unpaid benefits unclaimed.
- **This Bill** ensures the money actually reaches the veteran’s family or estate, making it easier for survivors to receive what the veteran earned.

You can read the full text of the bill for more details: [Source: Congress.gov](https://www.congress.gov/119/bills/hr3123/BILLS-119hr3123eh.pdf)

If you have more questions or want to vote on the bill, you can get started here: [Vote on DDP](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote)

all_results.entity_name: Ban certain substances in school meals for student health and safety (HB 4369)
all_results.prompt:
I don't live in Michigan but I want to ask my state representatives if we have a bill that bans these substances in our schools. 

do you know the other states that have a law like this?
all_results.response_text:
That’s a great question! The **Ban certain substances in school meals for student health and safety (HB 4369)** is a Michigan bill aiming to prohibit specific additives and dyes in school foods starting July 1, 2028. The substances banned include brominated vegetable oil, potassium bromate, propylparaben, titanium dioxide, and dyes like Red 40, Yellow 6, Green 3, Blue 1, and Blue 2 [[Source: Digital Democracy Project](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/ban-certain-substances-in-school-meals-for-student-health-and-safety-hb4369-2025)].

### Do Other States Have Similar Laws?

- **Currently, there is no single federal law** in the U.S. that bans these specific substances in all school meals.
- **California** passed a law in 2023 (the “California Food Safety Act”) banning the manufacture, sale, or distribution of foods containing brominated vegetable oil, potassium bromate, propylparaben, and Red 3, but it does not specifically mention school meals.
- **Other states** are considering similar legislation, but as of now, Michigan is among the first to propose a comprehensive ban specifically targeting school meals.

#### What You Can Do:
- You can check if your state has introduced similar bills by searching your state’s legislative website or contacting your state representatives.
- If you’re interested in encouraging similar action, you can share information about the Michigan bill and the substances it addresses with your representatives.

all_results.entity_name: Ban certain substances in school meals for student health and safety (HB 4369)
all_results.prompt: Can you look up Texas
all_results.response_text:
Yes, Texas has enacted legislation to prohibit certain food additives in school meals. Senate Bill 314 (SB 314), passed in 2025, amends the Education Code to ban specific substances from free or reduced-price meals provided by public school districts and open-enrollment charter schools. ([capitol.texas.gov](https://capitol.texas.gov/tlodocs/89R/analysis/html/SB00314H.htm?utm_source=openai))

**Key Provisions of SB 314:**

- **Prohibited Additives:** The bill lists several substances that cannot be included in school meals, including:
  - Brominated vegetable oil (BVO)
  - Potassium bromate
  - Propylparaben
  - Azodicarbonamide
  - Butylated hydroxyanisole (BHA)
  - Red 3
  - Red 40
  - Yellow 5
  - Yellow 6
  - Blue 1
  - Blue 2
  - Green 3
  - Caramel (CAS 8028-89-5)
  - Titanium dioxide
  - Any additive substantially similar to those listed

- **Implementation Timeline:**
  - The prohibition applies beginning with the 2026-2027 school year.
  - It affects contracts for providing free or reduced-price meals entered into or renewed on or after September 1, 2026. ([capitol.texas.gov](https://capitol.texas.gov/tlodocs/89R/analysis/html/SB00314H.htm?utm_source=openai))

This legislative action aligns with a broader movement to enhance the nutritional quality of school meals and protect student health by eliminating potentially harmful additives.

For more details, you can refer to the official bill text: ([capitol.texas.gov](https://capitol.texas.gov/tlodocs/89R/billtext/html/SB00314I.htm?utm_source=openai))

If you're interested in advocating for similar legislation in your state, consider reaching out to your state representatives to discuss the importance of such measures for student health and safety.

all_results.entity_name: Approval process for acceptance of gifts and funding for election administration (HB 639)
all_results.prompt: How would this bill change the way things currently are?
all_results.response_text:
Great question! Here’s how [Elections administration acceptance of certain gifts and funding approval required (HB 639)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-administration-acceptance-of-certain-gifts-and-funding-approval-required-hb639-2026) would change current practices in Virginia:

---

## **Current Practice**
- **Election offices** (state and local) can receive gifts, grants, or services from private individuals or organizations to help fund:
  - Voter education and outreach
  - Voter registration
  - Other election administration costs
- There may be general oversight, but there isn’t a strict, formal approval process for larger private contributions.

---

## **What HB 639 Would Change**
- **Limits Large Gifts:** Election offices could **not** accept, use, or solicit gifts, grants, property, or services **over $1,000** from private individuals or groups unless:
  - **A two-thirds majority vote** of all members of the State Board or local governing body approves it.
- **Adds Oversight:** This means any significant private funding would need broad, public approval before it could be used.
- **Allows Exceptions:**
  - **Private buildings** can still be used as polling places or satellite offices.
  - **Federal grants** (even those containing private donations) are still allowed.

---

## **Impact**
- **Increases oversight** of private money in election administration, aiming to prevent undue influence.
- **Could slow down or limit** access to extra resources for voter outreach and election needs, since approval is more difficult.

---

For more details and to see the bill’s text, visit the [bill’s DDP page](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-administration-acceptance-of-certain-gifts-and-funding-approval-required-hb639-2026) or check the [official legislative text](https://drive.google.com/uc?id=1ROr9ZObX8kQDMQSAbVbUu8ScI0GlhWnI&export=download).

**Want to weigh in on this bill?** You can get started here: [Vote on DDP](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote)

---

## **Impact Statement: Voting Yes on HB 639**

A **yes vote** would:

- **Increase Oversight of Private Funding:**
  - Election offices would need approval from a two-thirds majority of the State Board or local governing body before accepting gifts, grants, or services over $1,000 from private individuals or organizations.
- **Limit Immediate Access to Private Support:**
  - Election offices would no longer be able to quickly accept large private donations for voter education, registration, or other election expenses without this public approval.
- **Aim to Prevent Undue Influence:**
  - Supporters argue this change could reduce the risk of outside groups having too much influence over how elections are run.
- **Preserve Certain Exceptions:**
  - The bill still allows the use of privately owned buildings as polling places or satellite offices.
  - Federal grants, even those containing private donations, would still be permitted.

**In summary:**  
A yes vote is intended to add a layer of transparency and public oversight to the acceptance of significant private funding in election administration. This could help build trust in the electoral process but may also slow or limit extra support for election offices, especially for outreach or registration efforts.

Great question! Here’s a comparison between what [HB 639](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-administration-acceptance-of-certain-gifts-and-funding-approval-required-hb639-2026) proposes and the current law in Virginia:

---

## **Current Law**
- **Election offices** (state and local) can generally accept gifts, grants, money, property, or services from private individuals or organizations to support voter education, registration, and other election functions.
- There are **no specific requirements** for a supermajority public vote before accepting gifts above a certain amount from private sources.
- Oversight mechanisms exist, but **approval thresholds** for accepting private support are not as high or explicit.

---

## **Changes Proposed by HB 639**
- **Limits on Large Private Support:** Election offices **cannot accept, use, or dispose of** gifts, grants, property, or services **over $1,000** from private individuals or nongovernmental entities **unless approved by a two-thirds majority** of the State Board or local governing body.
- **Public Approval Requirement:** Adds a **formal, public approval process** for significant private contributions to election offices.
- **Clarifies Exceptions:**
  - Still allows use of private buildings as polling places or satellite offices.
  - Still allows acceptance of federal grants (even if those grants are funded by private donations).

---

**In summary:**  
The main change is adding a **stricter approval process** for large private donations—requiring a supermajority vote—whereas current law does not require such a high level of public approval for these contributions.

For more details, you can see the [bill’s DDP page](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-administration-acceptance-of-certain-gifts-and-funding-approval-required-hb639-2026) or review the [official legislative text](https://drive.google.com/uc?id=1ROr9ZObX8kQDMQSAbVbUu8ScI0GlhWnI&export=download).

[Source: Digital Democracy Project](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-administration-acceptance-of-certain-gifts-and-funding-approval-required-hb639-2026)

all_results.prompt: does this bill ban RCV?
all_results.response_text:
## Does the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act (HR22) Ban Ranked Choice Voting (RCV)?

The [Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act (HR22)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/save-act-hr22-2025) is focused on amending the National Voter Registration Act of 1993 to require proof of United States citizenship to register to vote in federal elections. Its key provisions include:

- **Requiring documentary proof of U.S. citizenship** for voter registration in federal elections
- **Specifying acceptable forms of proof** (e.g., passport, birth certificate, military ID)
- **Procedures for election officials** to notify and verify applicants
- **Removal of noncitizens from voter rolls**

### Is Ranked Choice Voting Addressed or Banned?

- The bill's text, based on all available sections, **does not mention or address Ranked Choice Voting (RCV)** or any other voting method besides requirements for voter registration and proof of citizenship.
- The bill is focused strictly on **eligibility and documentation for voter registration**, not election administration methods such as RCV, plurality, or runoff voting.

**Summary:**  
No, the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act (HR22) does not ban Ranked Choice Voting or make any changes to how ballots are counted or how elections are conducted beyond voter registration requirements. Its sole focus is proof of citizenship for federal election registration.

If you have more questions about the bill or its potential impacts, feel free to ask!  
You can also read the full bill text for details: [Source: Congress.gov](https://www.congress.gov/119/bills/hr22/BILLS-119hr22ih.pdf)

all_results.entity_name: Entering Virginia into the National Popular Vote (NPV) Compact (HB 965)
all_results.prompt: tell me about this
all_results.response_text:
## Overview: Entering Virginia into the National Popular Vote (NPV) Compact (HB 965)

**HB 965** is a bill that would have Virginia join the National Popular Vote (NPV) interstate compact. Here’s what that means and what’s in the bill:

### What Does the Bill Do?

- **Joins the National Popular Vote Compact:** If passed, Virginia would enter an agreement with other states to award its electoral votes in presidential elections to the candidate who wins the most votes nationwide—not just in Virginia.
- **How It Works:** The compact only takes effect once enough states join to control a majority of Electoral College votes (currently 270 out of 538).
- **Key Provisions:**
  - Sets deadlines for sharing vote totals among member states.
  - Establishes procedures for resolving ties and handling recounts.
  - Limits the ability for states to withdraw from the compact at the last minute.
  - Delays Virginia’s next change in how it awards electoral votes to the 2028 presidential election.

### Why Supporters Favor It

- **Every Vote Counts Equally:** Ensures that every vote across the country is weighed the same, regardless of state.
- **Nationwide Campaigns:** Encourages presidential candidates to campaign in all states, not just battleground or “swing” states.

### Why Opponents Object

- **Preserves Virginia’s Autonomy:** Opponents want Virginia to continue awarding its electors based solely on the state’s results.
- **Concerns About Multi-State Coordination:** Some worry the compact could create disputes or recounts across state lines and undermine the traditional Electoral College system.

### Sponsors and Status

- **Primary Sponsor:** Marcia S. "Cia" Price
- **Co-Sponsors:** Dan I. Helmer, Jessica L. Anderson, Bonita G. Anthony, Elizabeth B. Bennett-Parker, and many others.
- **Current Status:** As of February 6, 2026, the bill was recommended for reporting with amendments by a subcommittee (5 Yes, 3 No). It’s moving through the legislative process in the Virginia House of Delegates.

### Learn More & Track Progress

- [Bill Page on Digital Democracy Project](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/national-popular-vote-compact-enters-virginia-into-an-interstate-compact-hb965-2026)
- [Official Legislative Text (PDF)](https://drive.google.com/uc?id=19ga0hnGs3Zm_eU7HySk-rbSm_FhwsPov&export=download)
- [Legislative History and Sponsors](https://openstates.org/va/bills/2026/HB965/)

If you’d like to tell your legislators how to vote on this bill, you can get started through the Digital Democracy Project app: [https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote)

**Let me know if you want more details about the bill or how the compact works!**

---

all_results.entity_name: Entering Virginia into the National Popular Vote (NPV) Compact (HB 965)
all_results.prompt: does this require a constitutional amendment?
all_results.response_text:
### Does HB 965 Require a Constitutional Amendment?

**No, HB 965 does not require a constitutional amendment**—either at the federal or Virginia state level.

#### Why Not?

- **State Authority Over Electors:**  
  The U.S. Constitution (Article II, Section 1) gives each state legislature the power to decide how its presidential electors are chosen. States may assign their electors by any method they choose, including joining an interstate compact like the National Popular Vote (NPV) agreement.
- **How the Compact Works:**  
  HB 965 would have Virginia join the NPV compact, promising to award its electoral votes to the national popular vote winner once enough states join to guarantee a nationwide majority. This change is made by regular state legislation, not by amending constitutions.

#### What Would Require an Amendment?

- **Abolishing the Electoral College:**  
  If the goal were to eliminate the Electoral College entirely and elect the President directly by national popular vote, that would require a federal constitutional amendment.
- **Changing Virginia’s Method Permanently:**  
  If Virginia wanted to enshrine this system in its own state constitution (rather than just in law), a state constitutional amendment could be pursued, but that is not required here.

#### In Summary

- **HB 965 uses the existing power of the Virginia General Assembly to decide how electors are appointed.**
- **No constitutional amendment is needed to join the National Popular Vote Compact.**

You can read the bill text and see how it outlines this process here:  
[National Popular Vote Compact enters Virginia into an interstate compact (HB 965)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/national-popular-vote-compact-enters-virginia-into-an-interstate-compact-hb965-2026)  
[Source: Official Legislative Text (PDF)](https://drive.google.com/uc?id=19ga0hnGs3Zm_eU7HySk-rbSm_FhwsPov&export=download)

all_results.entity_name: PELOSI Act (S1498)
all_results.prompt: Hi Votebot - it looks like s1498 was updated and has a new bill text and new name
all_results.response_text:
You're absolutely right! The bill S1498 has gone through some updates, including a change in its official name and recent amendments.

## Current Name and Status

- **Official Title:** PELOSI Act (Preventing Elected Leaders from Owning Securities and Investments Act)
- **Alternate Title:** Halting Ownership and Non-Ethical Stock Transactions (HONEST) Act
- **Current Status:** The bill has been reported out of the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs with an amendment in the nature of a substitute and is now placed on the Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders (Calendar No. 294) as of December 10, 2025.  
  [Source: US Congress](https://openstates.org/us/bills/119/S1498/)

## What the Bill Does

- **Purpose:** Prohibits Members of Congress and their spouses from trading or holding certain stocks and financial instruments while in office, aiming to prevent conflicts of interest.
- **Divestment Requirement:** Current members and their spouses must divest disallowed investments within 180 days of enactment; new members have 180 days from taking office.
- **Enforcement:** Noncompliance can result in fines or forfeiture of profits. Ethics committees oversee compliance and publish certifications online for transparency.
  [Source: Digital Democracy Project](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/preventing-elected-leaders-from-owning-securities-and-investments-act-s1498-2025)

## Recent Actions

- 2025-12-10: Reported by Senator Paul with an amendment in the nature of a substitute; placed on the calendar.
- 2025-07-30: Ordered to be reported favorably with an amendment.
- 2025-04-28: Introduced in the Senate.

## Sponsors

- **Primary Sponsor:** Senator Josh Hawley
- **Co-Sponsors:** Bernie Moreno, Jon Ossoff, Gary Peters, Jeff Merkley  
  [Source: Digital Democracy Project](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/preventing-elected-leaders-from-owning-securities-and-investments-act-s1498-2025)

---

all_results.entity_name: Approval process for acceptance of gifts and funding for election administration (HB 639)
all_results.prompt: Can you provide more info on why the League of Women Voters supports this bill?
all_results.response_text:
Certainly! The [League of Women Voters of Virginia](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/member-organizations/league-of-women-voters-of-virginia) is listed as a supporter of the [Elections administration acceptance of certain gifts and funding approval required (HB 639)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-administration-acceptance-of-certain-gifts-and-funding-approval-required-hb639-2026).

### Why Might the League Support HB 639?

While the League of Women Voters of Virginia has not published a specific statement on this bill in the materials provided, organizations that support HB 639 typically do so for the following reasons:

- **Promoting Transparency and Oversight:** The bill requires a two-thirds public vote by the State Board or local governing body before election offices can accept private donations over $1,000. This adds an extra layer of oversight and transparency to outside funding in elections.
- **Limiting Outside Influence:** By restricting large private gifts or grants unless there is broad approval, the bill aims to prevent undue influence from private individuals or organizations on election administration.
- **Maintaining Public Trust:** Supporters argue that stricter controls over private funding help maintain public confidence in the impartiality and integrity of election offices.
- **Allowing Flexibility Where Needed:** The bill still permits the use of private buildings as polling places and allows federal grants that include private donations, ensuring election operations can continue smoothly.

These points align with the League of Women Voters’ general advocacy for transparent, fair, and trustworthy elections.

For more details about the bill’s provisions and impact, you can review the [bill summary and full text here](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-administration-acceptance-of-certain-gifts-and-funding-approval-required-hb639-2026).

If you’d like to see how other organizations are engaging with this bill, or want to cast your own vote, you can get started at [digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote)!

If you want the League’s official statement or a deeper dive into their position, I recommend reaching out to them directly or checking their legislative advocacy updates.

all_results.entity_name: Public Employees Relations Commission (HB 995)
all_results.prompt: Can you explain what this means?: "Employers must give equal access during union drives."
all_results.response_text:
Absolutely! Here’s a clear explanation of what “employers must give equal access during union drives” means in the context of the [Public Employees Relations Commission (HB 995)](https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/995/BillText/Filed/PDF):

---

### What Does "Equal Access" Mean During Union Drives?

- **Union drives** are periods when public employees are deciding whether to be represented by a union (also called an employee organization) or when there are efforts to certify, recertify, or decertify a union as the employees’ bargaining agent.
- **Equal access** means that all parties involved in the union process—whether they support, oppose, or wish to intervene in the certification or decertification of a union—must be given the same opportunities by the employer.
- **This includes:**
  - Access to public employer facilities (like break rooms, bulletin boards, or meeting spaces) for meetings or communication
  - Access to the employer’s internal communication systems (such as email or intranet), when these are used for union-related purposes

---

### Why Is This Important?

- It ensures that the process is fair.
- No group (whether pro-union or anti-union) is given an unfair advantage in communicating with employees.
- Employees get balanced information to make an informed decision.

---

### What Does HB 995 Say?

According to the bill:
> The public employer must provide equal access to its facilities and internal communication means to any employee organization or public employee seeking to support, oppose, or intervene in union certification, recertification, or decertification drives—from the time a petition is filed until the final resolution of the petition.  
> [Source: Florida Senate](https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/995/BillText/Filed/PDF)

---

**Summary:**  
During union-related campaigns, public employers must ensure that all sides have the same opportunity to reach employees using the employer’s facilities and communication systems.

If you have more questions about this bill or want to tell your legislator how you’d like them to vote, you can get started here: [https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote)

all_results.entity_name: Entering Virginia into the National Popular Vote (NPV) Compact (HB 965)
all_results.prompt: I'd like to ask my legislator to amend this bill so that Virginia apportions electors by congressional district.  Can you draft a proposed amendment in legislative text format?
all_results.response_text:
Certainly! Here’s a **sample amendment in legislative text format** that would change how Virginia apportions its presidential electors—using congressional districts rather than joining the National Popular Vote (NPV) Compact. This language is styled to fit typical legislative drafting conventions and is designed to be added as a substitute or amendment to [HB 965](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/national-popular-vote-compact-enters-virginia-into-an-interstate-compact-hb965-2026).

---

### Proposed Amendment to HB 965: Congressional District Method for Elector Allocation

**Amend HB 965 by striking all after the enacting clause and inserting:**

> **§ 24.2-209.2. Appointment of presidential electors by congressional district.**
> 
> (A) In each year in which electors for President and Vice President of the United States are to be appointed, the qualified voters of each congressional district within the Commonwealth shall vote for one elector. The elector candidate who receives the highest number of votes in each congressional district shall be appointed as that district’s elector.
>
> (B) In addition to the electors chosen by congressional district, two electors shall be chosen on the basis of the statewide vote. The two elector candidates at-large who receive the highest number of votes statewide shall be appointed as the Commonwealth’s at-large electors.
>
> (C) The total number of electors appointed under this section shall equal the whole number of senators and representatives to which the Commonwealth is entitled in Congress.
>
> (D) This section supersedes any conflicting provision of law relating to the manner of appointing presidential electors.

---

### How This Works

- **1 elector per congressional district**: The candidate winning each district gets 1 elector.
- **2 at-large electors**: The statewide winner gets 2 additional electors.
- **Total matches Virginia’s electoral votes**: The sum equals Virginia’s seats in Congress.

---

If you’d like to contact your legislator, you can copy and share this text or tailor it to your preference.

For more details on [HB 965](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/national-popular-vote-compact-enters-virginia-into-an-interstate-compact-hb965-2026), visit the full bill page.

If you need help finding your legislator or want to submit this suggestion through Digital Democracy Project, you can get started here: https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote

all_results.entity_name: Va Residential Landlord and Tenant Act landlord remedies noncompliance with rental agreement (HB 15)
all_results.prompt: What's the latest status on this bill?
all_results.response_text:
As of February 12, 2026, the [Va Residential Landlord and Tenant Act landlord remedies noncompliance with rental agreement (HB 15)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/va-residential-landlord-and-tenant-act-landlord-remedies-noncompliance-with-rental-agreement-hb15-2026) is under consideration in the Virginia General Assembly. The bill proposes amendments to § 55.1-1245 of the Code of Virginia, focusing on landlord remedies for tenant noncompliance with rental agreements.

**Key Provisions of HB 15:**

- **Extended Notice Period for Nonpayment of Rent:** The bill increases the notice period that landlords must provide tenants for nonpayment of rent from 5 days to 14 days. This change allows tenants more time to address overdue rent before the landlord can initiate termination of the rental agreement.

- **Clarification on Nonremediable Breaches:** HB 15 specifies that certain breaches by tenants, such as illegal drug activity or other criminal acts posing a threat to health or safety, are considered nonremediable. In such cases, landlords may terminate the rental agreement immediately and seek possession of the premises.

- **Protections for Victims of Family Abuse:** The bill includes provisions to protect tenants who are victims of family abuse. If the perpetrator is barred from the dwelling unit, the lease shall not terminate solely due to an act of family abuse against the tenant, provided certain conditions are met.

The bill is currently in the committee stage, where it is being reviewed and discussed. The next steps involve potential amendments, committee votes, and, if approved, progression to the full House and Senate for further consideration.

For more detailed information and to track the bill's progress, you can visit the [Digital Democracy Project's page on HB 15](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/va-residential-landlord-and-tenant-act-landlord-remedies-noncompliance-with-rental-agreement-hb15-2026).

all_results.prompt: tell me about HB 1119
all_results.response_text:
Absolutely! Here’s a summary of [Materials Harmful to Minors (HB 1119)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/materials-harmful-to-minors-hb1119-2026), a 2026 Florida legislative bill:

---

## [Materials Harmful to Minors (HB 1119)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/materials-harmful-to-minors-hb1119-2026)

### Purpose
HB 1119 tightens control over school books and instructional materials in Florida’s K-12 public schools. It defines what is considered “harmful to minors,” expands who can object to school materials, and sets procedures for removing certain content.

### Key Provisions
- **Defines “harmful to minors”:**  
  Content that depicts nudity or sexual conduct appealing to prurient interest, and is considered patently offensive by adult community standards for minors.
- **Objection Process:**  
  - **Parents and county residents** can object to any item used in classrooms, libraries, or reading lists.
  - **Non-parent residents** may file up to one objection per month.
  - Objection forms and processes must be easy to find on district websites.
- **Removal of Materials:**
  - If a material is flagged as pornographic or harmful to minors, it must be removed from student access within 5 school days and stay unavailable until the objection is resolved.
  - Literary or artistic value **cannot** be used as a reason to keep flagged material.
- **Accountability:**  
  - The state can audit school districts for compliance.
  - The state may withhold funds or require corrective action if districts do not follow the law.
- **Public Involvement:**  
  - Hearings on objections must be open to the public and include parents on committees.

### Supporters Say
Vote YES if you want:
- Rapid removal of materials deemed harmful or pornographic.
- Stronger parental and community control over school content.
- Greater transparency and state oversight.

### Opponents Say
Vote NO if you want:
- To avoid mandatory book removals and possible censorship.
- To allow consideration of literary or artistic merit.
- To keep more discretion for educators and librarians.

### Bill Status
- Sponsored by: Bankson, Trabulsy (Primary); Kendall, Tramont, Yarkosky (Co-Sponsors)
- Last major action: Added to Special Order Calendar for House vote on 2/11/2026.

### Learn More

Absolutely! Here are three additional potential pros for the [Voter registration regular periodic review of registration records (HB 28)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/voter-registration-regular-periodic-review-of-registration-records-hb28-2026):

---

### Additional Pros of HB 28

- **Prevents Last-Minute Disenfranchisement**
  - By creating a 90-day quiet period before elections for large-scale voter roll removals, the bill helps ensure eligible voters aren’t unexpectedly removed from the rolls right before they vote. This gives voters greater confidence that their registration status will be stable as Election Day approaches.

- **Standardizes Procedures Across Localities**
  - HB 28 sets clear and uniform deadlines for local registrars to act on notices and for voters flagged as non-citizens to respond. This helps reduce confusion, ensures fairness, and makes election administration more predictable statewide.

- **Reduces Administrative Errors**
  - By giving local registrars more time (60 days instead of 30) to process state notices and voters more time (28 days instead of 14) to respond to citizenship challenges, the bill lowers the risk of mistakes or missed communications, which can lead to wrongful removals.

---

If you’d like to see the arguments for and against, or want to vote on this bill, you can do so here: [HB 28 on Digital Democracy Project](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/voter-registration-regular-periodic-review-of-registration-records-hb28-2026).

all_results.prompt: Great, now can you tell me 3 cons
all_results.response_text:
Of course! Here are three potential cons that have been raised about the [Voter registration regular periodic review of registration records (HB 28)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/voter-registration-regular-periodic-review-of-registration-records-hb28-2026):

---

### Potential Cons of HB 28

- **Delays in Removing Ineligible Voters**
  - By creating a 90-day quiet period before elections for large voter roll removals, the bill could delay the removal of individuals who have moved, died, or otherwise become ineligible, leaving outdated or inaccurate records on the voter rolls during an election.

- **Increased Administrative Burden**
  - The bill extends the amount of time local registrars must spend processing notices and responding to voter inquiries. This could place additional administrative and resource burdens on local election offices, especially in high-turnout election years.

- **Potential for Voter Confusion**
  - Changing deadlines and procedures for voter roll maintenance may lead to confusion among both voters and election officials. Voters who receive notices about their registration status may be unsure about new timelines or requirements, potentially increasing the risk of misunderstandings.

---

These are common concerns raised in discussions about voter roll maintenance reforms. all_results.entity_name: Extending deadlines for correcting absentee ballots (HB 773)
all_results.prompt: if this bill is removing the rule that ballots had to arrive by the friday before election day to be cured, then what would it be changing it to?
all_results.response_text:
Great question! Yes, [Elections absentee voting cure process (HB 773)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-absentee-voting-cure-process-hb773-2026) proposes an important change to the timeline for "curing" (fixing) mistakes on absentee ballots in Virginia.

### Current Rule (Before HB 773)
- Only absentee ballots **received by the Friday before Election Day** could be corrected (“cured”) if there was a problem with the ballot or the required affirmation statement.

### Proposed Change (HB 773)
- **Removes** the Friday deadline: Under HB 773, **any absentee ballot received (regardless of when it arrives, as long as it is on time)** can be corrected if there is an issue.
- **New Deadline for Curing:** Voters would have **until noon on the Monday after Election Day** to fix (cure) errors on their absentee ballot so it can be counted.
- **Prompt Notification:** Registrars must notify voters promptly if there’s a problem, giving them time to make corrections.

#### What This Means
- **More time and flexibility for voters** to correct mistakes.
- **More ballots can be counted** instead of being rejected for minor errors (like a missing middle initial or date).
- The process is intended to increase voter participation and accuracy.

You can read more details or track the bill’s progress here: [Elections absentee voting cure process (HB 773)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-absentee-voting-cure-process-hb773-2026)

### What Does “On Time” Mean for Absentee Ballots?

Regardless of the cure process, **absentee ballots must still meet Virginia’s standard deadline to be counted**. Typically, that means:

- **The ballot must be received by the general registrar’s office by 7:00 p.m. on Election Day.**
- If the ballot is mailed, Virginia law may allow it to be counted if postmarked on or before Election Day and received by noon on the Friday after Election Day. (Always check with your local election office for the most current rules!)

### New Cure Deadline Under HB 773

If there’s a problem with your absentee ballot (such as a missing signature or incomplete affirmation):

- **You would have until noon on the Monday after Election Day to correct the issue** so your ballot can be counted.

#### Example Timeline

- **Election Day**: Tuesday
- **Deadline to return absentee ballot**: Must be received by 7:00 p.m. on Election Day (or postmarked by Election Day and received by the following Friday, if applicable)
- **Deadline to fix (“cure”) ballot errors**: **Noon on the Monday after Election Day**

**Summary:**  
- Your ballot must be received on time according to Virginia’s absentee ballot deadlines.
- If there’s an error, you have until **noon on the Monday after Election Day** to correct it for your vote to count under HB 773.

- **Security:** Drop boxes are monitored and secured to protect ballots, and local election officials regularly collect and process them.

all_results.prompt: If this bill were to pass, could a voter drop a ballot off at any drop box on election day to still be counted, as long as it's in the drop box before 7 PM?
all_results.response_text:
Excellent question! Here’s how it would work if [HB 773](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-absentee-voting-cure-process-hb773-2026) passes:

---

### Returning Absentee Ballots via Drop Box on Election Day

- **Yes**, under current Virginia law—and this would continue if HB 773 passes—a voter can return their completed absentee ballot to an official drop box **by 7:00 p.m. on Election Day**, and it will be counted.
- The drop box must be an **official one** designated by your local general registrar’s office or at an approved polling location.
- As long as your ballot is **received in the drop box before the polls close at 7:00 p.m. on Election Day**, it meets the deadline for counting.

---

### What HB 773 Changes

- The bill doesn’t change the deadline for submitting absentee ballots, but it extends the time you have to “cure” (fix) certain mistakes on your ballot or envelope—**until noon on the Monday after Election Day**.
- This means, if you return your ballot on Election Day via drop box and there’s a correctable issue, you should be notified and have the chance to fix it by the new deadline.

---

### Summary

- **Drop your ballot in any official drop box before 7:00 p.m. on Election Day = on time and eligible to be counted.**
- **If there’s a correctable error, you’ll have until noon on the Monday after Election Day to fix it under HB 773.**

You can always check with your local registrar for the list of official drop box locations and any local instructions.

all_results.prompt: What does this mean "except for certain exemptions like law enforcement"?
all_results.response_text:
Great question! The phrase "except for certain exemptions like law enforcement" refers to specific exceptions in the bill for who can legally purchase firearms under the age limit.

### What the Exemption Means

- The bill generally **lowers the minimum age to purchase or transfer a firearm from 21 to 18 years old**.
- However, it specifically **exempts** certain individuals from these rules. This means the age restriction does **not** apply to:
  - **Law enforcement officers**
  - **Correctional officers**
  - **Servicemembers** (as defined by Florida law)

So, even if the minimum age were to remain 21 (under previous law), these exempted groups could purchase firearms at younger ages if required by their duties.

### Why This Matters

- The exemption is designed so that members of law enforcement, corrections, and the military can access firearms as needed for their official roles, regardless of the general age restriction for the public.

You can read the official legislative text for more details: [Minimum Age for Firearm Purchase or Transfer (HB 133)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/minimum-age-for-firearm-purchase-or-transfer-hb133-2026)

---

## Who Is Affected by the Bill?

- **General Public:**  
  - The bill would lower the minimum age to buy or receive a firearm from **21 to 18 years old** for most people.
  - Anyone **under 18** would still not be allowed to purchase or receive a firearm from licensed dealers.

## What Are the Exemptions?

- **Law Enforcement Officers**
- **Correctional Officers**
- **Servicemembers** (military personnel as defined by Florida law)

These groups are **exempt** from the age restriction. This means:
- **Even if the minimum age is 21 (under current law) or 18 (if the bill passes), members of these groups can purchase firearms regardless of their age.**
- The exemption is in place because these roles may require the use of firearms as part of official duties, and the law ensures they have legal access as needed.

## Why Is This Included?

- The exemption recognizes that **public safety and defense roles** may require younger individuals to be armed for their jobs.
- It allows these professionals to lawfully obtain firearms for work or service, independent of the age limit applied to the general public.

## Key Takeaway

all_results.prompt: Would this bill end voting by mail?
all_results.response_text:
## Does HB 991 End Voting by Mail?

**No, [Elections (HB 991)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-hb991-2026) does NOT end voting by mail in Florida.** However, the bill proposes several changes that affect how vote-by-mail ballots are handled, especially regarding voter eligibility and verification.

### Key Points About Voting by Mail in HB 991

- **Vote-by-mail ballots remain available:** The bill does not eliminate or prohibit voting by mail.
- **Additional Verification for Some Voters:** If a voter’s eligibility or citizenship is in question, their vote-by-mail ballot will be treated as a provisional ballot. This means:
  - The ballot will not be counted unless the voter provides required proof of eligibility within a set timeframe ([Source: Florida Senate](https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/991/BillText/Filed/PDF)).
  - Voters flagged as "potentially ineligible" will receive instructions on how to resolve their status so their ballot can be counted ([Source: Florida Senate](https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/991/BillText/Filed/PDF)).
- **Notice and Appeals:** Voters whose eligibility is questioned will be notified and given the opportunity to appeal or provide documentation ([Source: Florida Senate](https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/991/BillText/Filed/PDF)).
- **No outright ban:** Nowhere in the bill text is there a provision to ban or end vote-by-mail for eligible voters.

### Summary

- **Voting by mail will continue** for eligible voters under HB 991.
- The bill adds **new verification steps** for some voters, but does not eliminate the option.

If you have other questions about this bill or want to see how other voters feel about it, you can get started here: [https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote)

all_results.entity_name: Election Integrity (HB 991)
all_results.prompt: What does it mean by "must use paper ballots"?
all_results.response_text:
## What Does “Must Use Paper Ballots” Mean in HB 991?

The [Elections (HB 991)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-hb991-2026) bill includes language requiring that all voting in Florida be done using **paper ballots**—specifically, “marksense ballots.” Here’s what that means:

---

### Key Points

- **Paper Ballots Required:** All votes must be cast on a physical, paper ballot. This ballot is marked by the voter—usually by filling in ovals or connecting lines using a pen or marker.
- **Marksense Ballots Defined:** A marksense ballot is a type of paper ballot that is read by a scanning machine (optical scanner) to count the votes. Voters physically mark their choices, and the machine reads those marks ([Source: Florida Senate](https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/991/BillText/Filed/PDF)).
- **No Electronic-Only Voting:** The bill prohibits the use of fully electronic voting systems that do not produce a paper ballot. Every vote must have a paper trail.
- **Accessibility:** Voters with disabilities may use a “voter interface device” (like an accessible touchscreen or machine) to mark their ballot, but the device must produce a paper ballot that can be verified by the voter and scanned for counting ([Source: Florida Senate](https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/991/BillText/Filed/PDF)).
- **Why Paper Ballots?** The move to paper ballots is intended to:
  - Improve election security
  - Allow for reliable audits and recounts
  - Ensure there is a physical record of every vote cast

---

### Summary

- **All voting must use a physical, paper ballot that can be counted and audited.**
- These changes are designed to increase transparency and trust in the election process.

all_results.entity_name: Election Integrity (HB 991)
all_results.prompt: Which kinds of electronic voting currently occur in FL but would end if this bill were to pass?
all_results.response_text:
## Which Electronic Voting Methods Would End Under HB 991?

[Elections (HB 991)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-hb991-2026) would **end certain types of fully electronic voting in Florida**, but not all forms of electronic assistance at the polls. Here’s a breakdown:

---

### **Current Electronic Voting Methods in Florida**

1. **Direct-Recording Electronic (DRE) Voting Machines**
   - These devices allow voters to make selections on a touchscreen.
   - In some cases, votes are stored electronically **without producing a paper ballot**.
   - Some DREs have a "voter-verifiable paper audit trail" (VVPAT), but others do not.

2. **Voter Interface Devices (for Accessibility)**
   - Touchscreen or other accessible machines used by voters with disabilities.
   - These devices **produce a paper ballot** or printout that can be scanned and audited.

3. **Optical Scan Systems**
   - Voters mark a paper ballot, which is then scanned electronically.
   - The original paper ballot is retained for audits.

---

### **What Would End Under HB 991?**

- **Fully Electronic Voting Without Paper Ballots:**  
  The bill would ban voting systems that do not produce a physical, paper ballot for every vote cast.
    - **DRE machines that do NOT create a paper ballot** would no longer be allowed ([Source: Florida Senate](https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/991/BillText/Filed/PDF)).
    - All voting must be done using "official marksense ballots"—meaning a paper ballot marked by hand or by a device, then scanned.

---

### **What Stays Allowed?**

- **Voter Interface Devices for Accessibility:**  
  Voters with disabilities can still use accessible machines, **as long as the device produces a paper ballot** that can be verified and scanned ([Source: Florida Senate](https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/991/BillText/Filed/PDF)).
- **Optical Scanners and Paper Ballots:**  
  All voters will use paper ballots that are scanned and tabulated electronically.

---

### **Summary Table**

| Voting Method                                | Allowed if HB 991 Passes? |
|----------------------------------------------|:-------------------------:|
| DREs without paper trail                     | ❌ No                     |
| Touchscreen/Accessible with paper output      | ✅ Yes                    |
| Hand-marked/scanner paper ballots            | ✅ Yes                    |
| Online/Internet voting                       | ❌ No (already not allowed) |

---

**In short:**  
*Any voting method that does not produce a physical, auditable paper ballot for each voter would end if HB 991 becomes law.*

Want to share your opinion on this bill? Get started here: [https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote)

If HB 991 becomes law, it would require stricter proof of citizenship for voter registration and for some already-registered voters. The state would use records from the Department of Highway Safety and Motor Vehicles to verify whether someone is a U.S. citizen. If your citizenship can’t be confirmed through those records, you would need to provide documents—like a U.S. passport, birth certificate, or other accepted proof—to your local supervisor of elections. If you can’t provide the necessary documents, you would be listed as an “unverified voter” and would not be allowed to vote until your status is confirmed. If this situation comes up while you’re trying to vote, you could cast a provisional ballot, but it would only be counted if you submit proper proof of citizenship within a certain time after the election.

In addition to the citizenship requirements, the bill also says all voting in Florida must use paper ballots. Any method of voting that doesn’t create a physical, paper ballot for each voter would no longer be allowed. This means touchscreen machines that only store votes electronically and do not print out a paper ballot you can check would be banned. However, you would still be able to vote by filling out a paper ballot by hand and feeding it into a scanner, or—if you need accessibility help—using a special device that prints a paper ballot for you to review and scan. Every vote would have a paper record, making it possible to do recounts and audits. Online voting or fully electronic voting without a paper trail would remain prohibited. All these changes are meant to make elections more secure and transparent by ensuring voter eligibility and keeping a physical record of every vote.

HB 991 tightens election rules. Voters must swear they read instructions and are U.S. citizens. all_results.response_text:
Absolutely! Here’s an expanded, easy-to-understand summary of HB 991:

---

**HB 991 makes several changes to Florida’s election laws:**

- **Stricter Voter Registration:**  
  When you register to vote, you must swear that you read all the instructions and that you are a U.S. citizen. The state will use DMV (Department of Highway Safety and Motor Vehicles) records to check if you’re a citizen.

- **Proof of Citizenship Required:**  
  If the DMV can’t confirm your citizenship, you’ll be registered as an “unverified voter.” This means you cannot vote until you give your local elections office extra proof—like a passport or birth certificate. If this happens on Election Day, you can cast a provisional ballot, but it will only count if you provide proof of citizenship soon after.

- **Ongoing Checks:**  
  The state will regularly review voter records and share voter data with the courts and DMV to make sure only eligible citizens remain registered.

- **Paper Ballots and Audits:**  
  All voting must use paper ballots, either filled out by hand or printed by a machine you can review. Electronic-only voting (without a paper record) will not be allowed. After elections, automated audits will check that votes were counted correctly, and in very close races, officials will manually review ballots.

- **Ban on Foreign Donations & Tougher Penalties:**  
  The bill bans campaign donations from foreign sources and increases penalties for breaking election laws. There’s also a five-year time limit for prosecuting election fraud.

- **Citizen Status on IDs:**  
  Florida driver’s licenses and ID cards will show if someone is a U.S. citizen.

---

These changes are intended to make Florida’s elections more secure and accurate by making sure only eligible citizens can vote, requiring a paper record of every vote, and increasing penalties for election violations.

[Learn more about HB 991](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/elections-hb991-2026)

all_results.response_text:
Absolutely! Here’s a clear, expanded summary of the [Systems of Law and Terrorist Organizations (HB 1471)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/systems-of-law-and-terrorist-organizations-hb1471-2026) bill in easy-to-understand language:

---

**HB 1471** is a bill that aims to strengthen Florida’s laws about terrorism and clarify how different legal systems can be used in state courts. Here’s what the bill would do:

- **Limits on Foreign and Religious Law:** The bill says Florida courts, agencies, and officials can’t apply foreign or religious laws in a way that takes away anyone’s rights under the U.S. or Florida Constitutions. This means your constitutional rights always come first, no matter what outside law is being considered.

- **Defining Domestic Terrorist Organizations:** It creates an official definition for “domestic terrorist organization.” The Chief of Domestic Security would have the power to label certain groups as domestic or foreign terrorist organizations if they meet specific criteria, such as engaging in dangerous, illegal acts intended to intimidate people or influence government by force.

- **Criminal Penalties:** Anyone who knowingly helps, joins, or provides resources to a designated terrorist organization could face felony charges, with harsher penalties if their actions cause serious injury or death.

- **State Support Restrictions:** The bill would ban state agencies, local governments, and public schools from using public funds to support organizations labeled as domestic or foreign terrorist organizations.

- **Education Provisions:** Private schools that want to participate in Florida’s school choice scholarship programs must ensure they do not employ or contract with anyone connected to a terrorist organization. Public colleges and universities would have to expel students who promote terrorist groups and make them ineligible for in-state tuition and financial aid.

- **Oversight and Updates:** The Chief of Domestic Security must keep an updated list of designated terrorist organizations, review these designations every five years, and publish the list publicly. Groups can challenge their designation in court and ask for removal if circumstances change.

In summary, this bill is designed to make it harder for terrorist organizations to operate in Florida, protect constitutional rights in court, and ensure that public resources and educational opportunities are not used to support or promote terrorism.

If you’d like to share your opinion on this bill, you can get started here: [https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote)

---

The bill gives the Chief of Domestic Security the authority to officially label certain groups as either domestic or foreign terrorist organizations if they are found to be engaging in dangerous or illegal activities that threaten public safety, such as acts intended to intimidate or influence the government by force. Anyone who knowingly helps, joins, or provides resources to a designated terrorist organization could face serious criminal penalties, including felony charges, especially if their actions result in serious injury or death. The bill would also prohibit state agencies, local governments, and public schools from spending public money to support these designated terrorist organizations. For private schools that want to participate in Florida’s school choice scholarship programs, the bill requires them to ensure that no one connected to a terrorist organization is employed or involved. In colleges and universities, students who promote terrorist groups could be expelled and lose eligibility for in-state tuition or financial aid. The Chief of Domestic Security would also be required to keep an updated list of designated terrorist organizations, review these designations regularly, and publish them for the public. Groups can challenge their designation in court and request removal if circumstances change. Overall, the bill is intended to make it harder for terrorist organizations to operate in Florida, ensure public resources aren’t used to support them, and protect everyone’s constitutional rights in the legal system.

If you’d like to make your voice heard on this bill, you can get started here: [https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote)

all_results.entity_name: Systems of Law and Terrorist Organizations (HB 1471)
all_results.prompt: How does it get determined who may be labeled as a domestic or foreign terrorist organizations?
all_results.response_text:
Great question! Under the [Systems of Law and Terrorist Organizations (HB 1471)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/systems-of-law-and-terrorist-organizations-hb1471-2026), the process for labeling an organization as a domestic or foreign terrorist organization involves several specific steps and criteria:

### How Organizations Are Designated

- **Authority:** The Chief of Domestic Security has the power to designate organizations as "domestic terrorist organizations" or "foreign terrorist organizations."
- **Criteria for Domestic Terrorist Organizations:**  
  - The organization must be based or operate in Florida or the United States.
  - It must engage in terrorist activities that:
    - Involve acts dangerous to human life and violate state or federal law, or
    - Are intended to intimidate or coerce civilians, influence government policy by intimidation or coercion, or affect government conduct through mass destruction, assassination, or kidnapping.
  - The organization’s terrorist activity must be an ongoing threat to Florida or the United States.

- **Criteria for Foreign Terrorist Organizations:**  
  - The organization must be officially designated as a foreign terrorist organization by the U.S. Secretary of State.
  - Its activities must represent an ongoing threat to Florida or the United States.

### The Designation Process

- The Chief of Domestic Security must provide written notice and findings to the Florida Cabinet at least seven days before making a designation.
- The Cabinet votes to approve or reject the proposed designation.
- Once approved, the designation is published in the Florida Administrative Register.
- The organization (or its members) can challenge the designation in court within 30 days of publication.
- The Chief of Domestic Security must review each designation at least every five years and maintain a public list of designated organizations.

### Review and Removal

- Organizations may petition for removal of their designation at any time.
- The Cabinet may also remove a designation by a majority vote.

This process is designed to ensure both government oversight and opportunities for judicial review [Source: Official Legislative Text](https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/1471/BillText/Filed/PDF).

If you’d like to engage with this bill or vote on it, you can get started here: [https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote)

all_results.entity_name: Complaints Against Law Enforcement and Correctional Officers (HB 1283)
all_results.prompt: Can you expand this summary, keeping it in paragraph format, but making it easier to understand?
all_results.response_text:
Absolutely! Here’s an expanded, easy-to-understand summary of the [Complaints Against Law Enforcement and Correctional Officers (HB 1283)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/complaints-against-law-enforcement-and-correctional-officers-hb1283-2026):

This bill sets new rules for how complaints against police officers and correctional officers are handled in Florida. It says that if someone wants to file a complaint against an officer, they generally must write it down and sign it under oath—unless they have other evidence to support their claim. If an officer is being investigated, they must be given a copy of the complaint before any questioning or disciplinary action can happen, so they know exactly what they’re being accused of. The bill also says that an officer can’t be suspended, demoted, or fired unless they have received a signed copy of the complaint. If an investigation doesn’t end with any disciplinary action, the officer’s chances for a promotion, raise, or award won’t be affected by that investigation. Anyone who files a false complaint could be penalized. Until a final decision is made about whether to discipline the officer, the details of the complaint and the investigation must stay confidential. The goal of the bill is to make the complaint process more formal and ensure officers have due process, while also setting consequences for false accusations.

If you’d like to see more details or review the official bill text, you can visit the [bill page here](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/complaints-against-law-enforcement-and-correctional-officers-hb1283-2026).

## Summary: Commercial Service Airports (HB 919)

This bill gives the **State of Florida** (not local airport boards) the authority to decide the names of Florida’s major commercial service airports. It sets official names for these airports, including renaming West Palm Beach’s airport to “Donald J. Trump International Airport.”  

The bill requires the **Department of Transportation (DOT)** to:
- Check each year which airports qualify as major commercial service airports
- Notify lawmakers if any airport’s status changes
- Ensure that all government records created after July 1, 2026, use the official state-designated airport names

Even if an airport gets smaller and loses its “major” status, its state-given name will remain. Expect clearer branding across state records and some costs to update signs, maps, and websites.

**Key Points:**
- **DOT** stands for Department of Transportation
- State (not local) control over naming major airports
- Official names must be used in government documents after July 1, 2026
- West Palm Beach airport would be renamed “Donald J. Trump International Airport”
- Annual review of airport status by DOT

For more details or to participate as a voter, visit: https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote

all_results.prompt: Display bill text
all_results.response_text:
Certainly! Here are the key sections and main text of the [Veterans Readiness and Employment Improvement Act of 2025 (HR 980)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/veterans-readiness-and-employment-improvement-act-of-2025-hr980-2026):

---

## Veterans Readiness and Employment Improvement Act of 2025 (HR 980)  
**119th Congress**

### Key Provisions from the Official Bill Text

1. **Short Title**  
This Act may be cited as the “Veterans Readiness and Employment Improvement Act of 2025”.

2. **On-Campus Counseling Changes**  
Amends existing law to allow colleges to use non-VA staff for on-campus educational and vocational counseling for veterans.  
> “Elimination of requirement that on-campus educational and vocational counseling is provided by certain Department of Veterans Affairs employees.”  
([Source: Congress.gov](https://www.congress.gov/119/bills/hr980/BILLS-119hr980eh.pdf))

3. **Non-Degree Flight Training**  
Allows the VA Secretary to approve non-degree flight training courses as part of vocational rehabilitation programs for eligible veterans with service-connected disabilities (effective August 1, 2026).  
> “The Secretary may approve a rehabilitation program for a veteran … that includes the pursuit of a course of flight training other than one given by an educational institution of higher learning for credit toward a standard college degree the veteran is seeking.”  
([Source: Congress.gov](https://www.congress.gov/119/bills/hr980/BILLS-119hr980eh.pdf))

4. **Dedicated VA Contact Line and Local Contacts**  
Requires the VA to establish a dedicated telephone number for inquiries about these services and to list contact info on each regional office’s website.

5. **Timely Extension Decisions**  
Requires the VA Secretary to approve or deny a request for a program extension within 30 days and report annually on extension requests.

6. **Extension of Pension Payment Limits**  
Extends certain limits on veterans' pension payments to July 31, 2033.

---

all_results.prompt: Can you expand on this summary to make it easier to understand but keep it in the same paragraph format?
all_results.response_text:
Of course! Here’s a clearer summary in paragraph form:

The bill titled [Making further consolidated appropriations for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2026, and for other purposes (HR 7147)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/making-further-consolidated-appropriations-for-the-fiscal-year-ending-september-30-2026-and-for-other-purposes-hr7147-2026) has been voted on in both the House of Representatives and the Senate. In the House, the bill was brought up for a vote on January 22, 2026, and it was approved, with 220 members voting in favor, 207 against, and 4 not voting. After passing the House, the bill moved to the Senate, where a key vote took place on February 12, 2026. Although a majority of Senators (52) voted yes, and 47 voted no (with 1 not voting), the motion failed because cloture typically requires a higher threshold (usually 60 votes) to pass. all_results.prompt: Can you expand this summary to make it a little easier to understand but keeping it in the same format?
all_results.response_text:
Absolutely! Here’s an expanded and easy-to-understand summary of the SAVE America Act (HR 7296), keeping a clear, structured format:

---

## SAVE America Act (HR 7296) – Expanded Summary

**Purpose:**  
The SAVE America Act aims to change how voter registration works for federal elections in the United States by requiring people to show documentary proof that they are U.S. citizens when registering to vote.

---

### Key Provisions

- **Documentary Proof of Citizenship:**  
  - Anyone who wants to register to vote in a federal election must provide physical documents proving they are a U.S. citizen (like a passport or birth certificate).
  - States must verify that all new voter registrations include this proof.

- **Special Processes for Those Without Documents:**  
  - If someone does not have the standard documents, states must set up a process allowing the applicant to submit other types of evidence and sign an affidavit (a sworn statement) that they are a U.S. citizen.
  - Election officials can decide if this alternative evidence is enough, but must also sign a statement explaining why.

- **Mail-In and Absentee Voting:**  
  - Voters who register by mail must also provide documentary proof of citizenship.
  - Absentee voters must include a copy of approved photo identification both when requesting and submitting their absentee ballot.

- **State Responsibilities:**  
  - States must actively ensure only citizens are registered to vote, including checking databases (like those from the Department of Homeland Security or Social Security Administration) to confirm citizenship status.
  - States must inform residents about the new documentation requirements.

- **Removal of Non-Citizens from Voter Rolls:**  
  - If there is proof someone on the voter rolls is not a U.S. citizen, states are required to remove that person from the list of eligible voters.

- **Penalties and Enforcement:**  
  - There are criminal penalties for election officials who register someone without the required citizenship documents.
  - Individuals or groups can take legal action if these requirements are not followed.

- **Exceptions and Special Cases:**  
  - If a state does not require voter registration at all, it must still have a system to confirm citizenship before voting.
  - The law does not prevent people from casting provisional ballots if there is a dispute about their eligibility.

- **Implementation:**  
  - The Election Assistance Commission will provide guidance and create uniform affidavit forms for cases where alternative proof is needed.
  - The law would go into effect for all federal elections after it is enacted.

---

**Potential Impact:**  
If passed, this law would make citizenship documentation a nationwide requirement for registering to vote in federal elections, aiming to prevent non-citizens from being added to voter rolls.

---

If you’d like to learn more or share your opinion on the SAVE America Act (HR 7296), you can get started here: https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote

For additional details, you can review the full bill text [Source: Congress.gov](https://www.congress.gov/119/bills/hr7296/BILLS-119hr7296ih.pdf).

all_results.prompt: Does this bill require ALL voters to show photo ID in order to vote?
all_results.response_text:
Yes, the [SAVE America Act (HR 7296)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/save-america-act-hr7296-2026) would require all voters in federal elections to present a photo ID in order to vote.

Here’s how the requirement works:

### In-Person Voting
- Voters must present a **tangible (not digital) eligible photo identification document** at the time of voting.

### Absentee Voting
- Voters must include a **copy of their eligible photo identification document** both:
  - When requesting an absentee ballot
  - When submitting the absentee ballot

### What Qualifies as an “Eligible Photo Identification Document”?
- It must be issued by:
  - A state motor vehicle agency
  - A state or local election office
  - A Native tribal government
  - The U.S. Department of State
  - The Department of War or a branch of the Armed Forces
- The document must:
  - Contain a photograph of the individual
  - Indicate U.S. citizenship on the front (with some exceptions allowed if accompanied by other proof)
  - Include either a state-issued ID number or the last four digits of the person’s Social Security Number

There are some special rules and exceptions for certain states and unique circumstances, but the general rule is a photo ID requirement for all voters in federal elections as outlined above.

For more detail, you can review the bill text directly: [Source: Congress.gov](https://www.congress.gov/119/bills/hr7296/BILLS-119hr7296ih.pdf).

If you have more questions or want to vote on this bill, you can get started here: https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/vote

([legislate.dev](https://legislate.dev/bills/119/hr-7300?utm_source=openai))

- **Prohibition on Federal Agencies in Voter Registration:** Federal agencies are prohibited from engaging in voter registration or mobilization activities, except for providing citizenship information to state officials. ([legislate.dev](https://legislate.dev/bills/119/hr-7300?utm_source=openai))

all_results.prompt: How does the SAVE America Act bill differ to the Making American Elections Great Again Act?
all_results.response_text:
Absolutely! Here’s a structured comparison between the two bills based on the information in our database:

---

## SAVE America Act (HR 7296)

**Purpose:**  
To amend the National Voter Registration Act of 1993 to require proof of United States citizenship to register to vote in federal elections.

**Key Provisions:**
- **Documentary Proof of Citizenship:** Requires applicants to provide specific documents (e.g., REAL ID-compliant ID, U.S. passport, military ID, or government-issued photo ID indicating citizenship) when registering to vote in federal elections.
- **Photo Voter ID Requirement:** Requires voters to present a tangible (not digital) photo ID when voting in person and to include a copy with absentee ballots.
- **Verification & Removal:** Mandates states to verify citizenship status and remove noncitizens from official voter lists.
- **Federal Agency Data Sharing:** Requires federal agencies to share citizenship status information with state election officials upon request.
- **No Ban on Ranked Choice Voting:** The bill does not address or restrict ranked choice voting.
- **Provisional Ballots:** Preserves the right to cast a provisional ballot if citizenship can be verified later.
- **Effective Immediately:** Applies to all new registrations after enactment.

For more details, see the [SAVE America Act (HR 7296)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/save-america-act-hr7296-2026).  
[Source: Congress.gov](https://www.congress.gov/119/bills/hr7296/BILLS-119hr7296ih.pdf)

---

## Make Elections Great Again Act (HR 7300)

**Purpose:**  
To strengthen election security and integrity through voter identification, citizenship verification, and reforms to voting procedures.

**Key Provisions:**
- **Photo ID Requirement for In-Person Voting:** Requires voters to show valid photo ID. If unable, a provisional ballot can be cast, with three days to provide an ID or affidavit.
- **Proof of Citizenship for Registration:** Requires documentary proof of U.S. citizenship and last four digits of Social Security number with all voter registrations.
- **Centralized Voter Rolls:** Mandates states maintain a single, computerized voter registration list with unique identifiers for each voter and regular accuracy checks.
- **Limits on Mail-In Ballots:** 
  - Limits individuals to possessing no more than four mail-in ballots (except immediate family/caregivers).
  - Eliminates universal vote-by-mail—voters must request a mail ballot at least 30 days before elections.
  - Ballot envelopes must include USPS barcode for tracking.
  - Absentee/mail-in ballots can only be processed after polls close.
- **Voter-Verified Paper Ballots:** Requires use of paper ballots for audits.
- **Federal Agency Restrictions:** Prohibits most federal agencies from engaging in voter registration or mobilization, except for sharing citizenship info with states.
- **Information Sharing:** States must report evidence of voting fraud to the U.S. Attorney General.

For more details, see the [Make Elections Great Again Act (HR 7300)](https://digitaldemocracyproject.org/bills/make-elections-great-again-act-hr7300-2026).

---

## **Key Differences**

| Feature                              | SAVE America Act (HR 7296)     | Make Elections Great Again Act (HR 7300)   |
|---------------------------------------|---------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Proof of Citizenship to Register      | Yes                            | Yes                                        |
| Photo ID to Vote                     | Yes (tangible, not digital)     | Yes (with provisional ballot option)        |
| Centralized Voter Rolls              | Not specified                   | Yes                                        |
| Limits on Possession of Mail Ballots  | Not specified                   | Yes (max 4 per person)                     |
| Universal Vote-by-Mail                | Not addressed                   | Eliminated                                 |
| Ballot Tracking Barcode               | Not addressed                   | Required                                   |
| Processing of Absentee Ballots        | Not addressed                   | Only after polls close                      |
| Voter-Verified Paper Ballots          | Not addressed                   | Required                                   |
| Federal Agency Role in Registration   | Data sharing only               | Prohibited (with exception for citizenship info) |
| Ranked Choice Voting                  | Not addressed                   | Not addressed                              |

---

**Summary:**  
Both bills focus on citizenship verification and photo ID requirements, but HR 7300 introduces broader reforms related to mail-in ballots, vote-by-mail procedures, centralized voter rolls, ballot tracking, and restrictions on federal agency involvement.

If you have questions about a specific provision or impact, feel free to ask!
